findings.description: SQL injection audit — VERIFIED: All 14 SQL execute() calls use parameterized queries (? placeholders). Zero instances of f-strings, string concatenation, or .format() in SQL statements. Verdict: SAFE.

Parameterized query verification (enumerated): save_prediction (line 122-146, 16 params), get_expired_predictions (line 154-157, 2 params), verify_prediction (line 166-169, 1 param; line 185-188, 3 params), get_predictions_by_status (line 194-197, 2 params), expire_unverifiable (line 206-209, 2 params), save_lesson (line 218-234, 9 params), get_lessons_for_entity (line 242-244, 2 params), get_lessons_by_root_cause (line 254-257, 2 params), get_entity_memory (line 268-270, 1 param), update_entity_memory (line 288-306, 10 params), get_calibration (line 314-317, 1 param), update_calibration (line 328-344, 9 params).

db_path is user-controllable via __init__ parameter. Directory creation uses mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) — safe. No path traversal check on db_path — a malicious path like '../../etc/passwd' would be accepted. Mitigation: parent directory creation is safe, and sqlite3 will simply create/open a file at the specified path.
WAL journal mode + 5s busy_timeout for concurrent access safety. Appropriate for multi-reader scenarios.
_serialize_json() helper (line 362-368) handles None → '[]', str passthrough, and json.dumps for other types. No XSS risk since data is stored in SQLite not rendered as HTML.

notes: All SQL uses parameterized queries — confirmed safe from SQL injection. The db_path concern is mitigated by the fact that this is a server-side storage module where the path is controlled by config, not user input. WAL mode provides good concurrent read performance. Consider adding path sanitization if db_path ever comes from user input in the future.
